one_line_takeaway: FaLW reweights each forgotten sample's unlearning loss using a per-class Gaussian z-score of its predictive probability (further sharpened for tail classes via a class-frequency balance factor), cutting the Avg. Gap to Retrain from SalUn's 2.14-2.45 down to 0.35-0.40 on CIFAR-10 and Tiny-ImageNet long-tailed forget sets.
key_finding: On VGG-16/CIFAR-10 (10% forget rate, gamma=1), FaLW achieves Avg. Gap 0.35 (std 0.20) versus Retrain, compared to SalUn's 2.45 (std 0.41), SFRon's 3.68, RL's 3.69, GA's 27.77, BE's 27.22, BS's 26.02, FT's 31.95, l1-sparse's 38.07, and IU's 38.18 - i.e., FaLW's forget/retain/test accuracies and MIA score are all closest to the Retrain gold standard (FA 25.70 vs Retrain 25.63; RA 99.98 vs 99.98; TA 49.11 vs 48.83; MIA 73.30 vs 74.36). On VGG-16/Tiny-ImageNet (40% forget rate, gamma=1/2), FaLW again has the lowest Avg. Gap at 0.40 (std 0.19) versus SalUn's 2.14, SFRon's 2.93, FT's 2.18, RL's 4.13, BS's 9.90, l1-sparse's 10.54, BE's 14.96, IU's 19.61, GA's 10.42. The paper additionally reports (Table 2/3, CIFAR-100/ResNet-18, 30% unlearning ratio, varying gamma from 1/4 to 2) that FaLW consistently narrows the Forgetting Accuracy gap versus Retrain relative to SalUn across head, mid, and tail classes, correcting both over-forgetting and under-forgetting depending on which deviation direction the baseline exhibits, with the largest relative correction on tail-class samples in highly imbalanced (large gamma) settings.
core_contribution: The paper (ICLR 2026, USTC et al.) introduces long-tailed machine unlearning as a new problem: it shows that real-world 'right to be forgotten' forget requests (e.g., a deleted user account) are naturally class-imbalanced, unlike the near-balanced forget sets produced by the random sampling used in prior unlearning benchmarks. Through empirical analysis (Proposition 1/2, Observation 1/2 on CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18) the authors identify two failure modes of existing approximate unlearning methods under long-tailed forget sets: (1) Heterogeneous Unlearning Deviation - models under-forget head-class samples while over-forgetting tail-class samples, and (2) Skewed Unlearning Deviation - the magnitude of unlearning error is disproportionately larger for tail classes. To address both, they propose FaLW (Forgetting-aware Loss reWeighting), a plug-and-play instance-wise loss reweighting scheme that: (a) estimates each sample's 'unlearning deviation' as a z-score of its current predictive probability against a Gaussian fit to the probability distribution of unseen same-class validation data, (b) converts this z-score into a Forgetting-Aware Weight via wi = 1 + sign(zi)*tanh(|zi|)^(1/eta) that increases loss weight for under-forgotten samples and drives it toward zero for over-forgotten samples, and (c) modulates the exponent with a class-frequency-based Balance Factor Bi = (Nf / (C*Nf,k))^tau so tail-class samples get sharper, more sensitive weight adjustments than head-class samples.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker-vs-defender threat model. The paper's concern is a privacy-compliance/utility problem: given a machine-learning model trained on data D and a forget request set Df (which the paper argues is realistically long-tailed, e.g., a deactivating user's activity records concentrated in a few categories), the goal is to make the model behave as if it had never seen Df (approximating a retrain-from-scratch gold standard) while preserving accuracy on the retain set Dr and test set. Membership Inference Attack (MIA) accuracy on the forget set is used only as one of four evaluation metrics for how thoroughly data was forgotten, not as an attack the paper mounts or defends against directly - MIA itself is treated as a black-box classifier probing forget-set membership, following Carlini et al. 2022's usage in unlearning evaluation.
limitations: The paper's own text does not include a dedicated limitations section in the extracted pages; based on the methodology text: (1) FaLW's deviation estimate depends on approximating each class's unseen-data probability distribution as a single Gaussian N(mu_c, sigma_c^2) estimated from a held-out validation set, an assumption whose accuracy is not separately validated against the true (non-Gaussian) shape of held-out probability distributions; (2) the method introduces two additional hyperparameters (temperature eta and balance-factor exponent tau) that must be tuned; (3) FaLW is evaluated as an add-on to existing gradient-based unlearning objectives (built on top of methods like SalUn) rather than as a fully independent unlearning algorithm, so its performance is bounded by the quality of the underlying base unlearning procedure; (4) evaluation is restricted to image classification benchmarks (CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet) with synthetic power-law forget-set construction, not a real-world long-tailed forget-request dataset; (5) reliance on retraining from scratch as the ground-truth reference for computing Unlearning Deviation and for Avg. Gap makes the evaluation itself computationally expensive and assumes retraining is a faithful gold standard.

quantitative_results: VGG-16/CIFAR-10, 10% forget, gamma=1: FaLW Avg. Gap 0.35 (std 0.20) vs SalUn 2.45 (std 0.41), Retrain reference FA=25.63 RA=99.98 TA=48.83 MIA=74.36
VGG-16/Tiny-ImageNet, 40% forget, gamma=1/2: FaLW Avg. Gap 0.40 (std 0.19) vs SalUn 2.14 (std 0.15), Retrain reference FA=65.94 RA=100.00 TA=78.62 MIA=34.06
VGG-16/Tiny-ImageNet: FaLW FA=65.64 (gap 0.30), RA=99.99 (gap 0.00), TA=78.01 (gap 0.61), MIA=33.36 (gap 0.70)
Worst baselines on CIFAR-10: IU Avg. Gap 38.18, l1-sparse 38.07, FT 31.95; on Tiny-ImageNet: IU 19.61, l1-sparse 10.54, GA 10.42

CIFAR-100/ResNet-18, 30% unlearning ratio, gamma swept 1/4 to 2: FaLW narrows head/mid/tail Forgetting-Accuracy gaps to Retrain relative to SalUn (Table 2/3), with largest relative improvement on tail classes at high gamma
